data.title: TSMC hits record high after Taiwan loosens fund investment limits; Taiex climbs 3.3
data.description:
• Shares of Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) rose to a record high on Friday after regulators loosened limits on single-stock investments, enabling fund managers to increase exposure to the world’s largest contract chipmaker
  • Shares of the chipmaker gained as much as 5.1% and the benchmark Taiex Index rose as much as 3.3%, the most in Asia.
  • Local equity funds and actively managed exchange-traded funds can invest up to 25% of their funds into listed companies whose weight exceeds 10% of the Taiwan Stock Exchange starting from Friday, Taiwan’s financial supervisory commission said on Thursday.
  • Previously, investment funds could only invest up to 10% of their total net asset value in one company. 
  • The move, according to JPMorgan Chase & Co., could draw more than $6B of inflows, Bloomberg reported.

ETFs linked to Taiwan include (FLTW), (EWT).

data.title: Getty Realty signals 2026 AFFO per share of $2.50-$2.52 backed by $125M under contract
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Getty Realty Corp. (GTY) Q1 2026

Management view

  • “I am pleased to report that Getty is off to a strong start in 2026, highlighted by a 13.1% year-over-year increase in our annualized base rent, a 6.8% increase in our AFFO per share and an increase to our full year 2026 earnings guidance.” (President, CEO & Director Christopher Constant)

  • “Year-to-date, we have invested more than $34 million at an initial cash yield of 8%.” (President, CEO & Director Constant) He also said Getty had “approximately $125 million of investments under contract” and “more than $170 million of unsettled forward equity” while “our $450 million revolver is completely undrawn.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)

  • “At quarter end, our lease portfolio included 1,186 net lease properties and 2 active redevelopment sites.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Robert Ryan) Ryan said occupancy (excluding active redevelopments) was 99.7%, weighted average lease term was 10.1 years, and trailing 12-month tenant rent coverage was 2.5x. (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan)

  • “For the first quarter of 2026, we reported AFFO per share of $0.63.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Brian Dickman) He added that “FFO and net income for the quarter were $0.69 and $0.43 per share, respectively.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Outlook

  • “We are increasing our full year 2026 AFFO per share guidance to a range of $2.50 to $2.52 from the prior range of $2.48 to $2.50.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • “Our guidance reflects the current run rate from our in-place portfolio with certain expense and credit loss variability and does not include any prospective investments or capital markets activities.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Pipeline terms in the prepared remarks and Q&A emphasized a mix “predominantly relationship sale leasebacks and development funding opportunities with new 15- to 20-year lease terms,” with initial cash yields “in the mid- to high 7% area.” (Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan)

Financial results

  • Dickman reported a quarter G&A efficiency metric: the ratio of G&A (excluding stock-based compensation and nonrecurring retirement costs) to cash rental and interest income “was 9.2% for the quarter ended March 31, 2026.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Leverage and liquidity were described as: net debt-to-EBITDA “was 5.1x or 4.2x, including the impact of unsettled forward equity,” fixed charge coverage was 4x, and Getty ended the quarter with “$1 billion of total unsecured notes outstanding” at a weighted average interest rate of 4.5% and weighted average maturity of 6 years, with “no debt maturities until June 2028.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Equity funding capacity highlighted: “we raised $130 million of new common equity in an overnight offering” on a forward basis, with “5.5 million shares subject to outstanding forward sales agreements” anticipated to raise “approximately $171.5 million,” alongside “more than $625 million of total liquidity.” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

Q&A

  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: asked what is driving investment pipeline momentum; President, CEO & Director Constant said “it’s a little bit all of the above” and pointed to more deal makers, more relationships, consolidation, and sale-leaseback demand as operators assess capital needs.

  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: asked about selectivity across sectors; President, CEO & Director Constant said “within the 4 sectors that we invest in, we’re equally excited about all 4 of them.”

  • Mitch Germain, Citizens JMP Securities, LLC: asked about platform efficiency; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman referenced “technology” and “process improvement,” adding that “net lease platforms are inherently very scalable.”

  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc.: asked about larger portfolio deals; President, CEO & Director Constant said the pipeline includes “a mix of what I would call midsized to larger portfolios.”

  • Upal Rana, KeyBanc Capital Markets Inc.: questioned capital strategy and potential additional capital raising; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said “I don’t see any change in strategy,” emphasizing leverage “in that 4.5 to 5.5x range,” revolver usage, forward equity settlement, and that incremental equity decisions depend on pipeline scale, pricing, and stock trading levels.

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank: asked about bad debt and assumptions in guidance; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said Getty used “about 25 basis points assumption for credit loss,” saw none in Q1, and said “there’s nothing that rises to a level of a watch list for us.”

  • Michael Goldsmith, UBS Investment Bank: asked about 7-Eleven store closures; President, CEO & Director Constant said the trend toward larger, food-forward stores “is not a new trend,” cited recent acquired average store size “like either 7,000 or 8,000 square feet,” and said renewed legacy leases reflect that “those still make money for our tenants.” Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan added 7-Eleven indicated “about 1/3 of those closures numerically as planned reopening or a new stores in that larger format.”

  • Brad Heffern, RBC Capital Markets: asked about the impact of “soaring gas prices”; President, CEO & Director Constant said prices started below $3 nationally, fuel margins began “north of $0.40, maybe $0.45,” and said “almost all of that increase has been passed on,” with margins still “nationally above $0.40.”

  • Brad Heffern, RBC Capital Markets: asked what drove the AFFO guidance increase; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman cited investment activity plus performance vs. assumptions, noting “we had no credit loss in the first quarter and expenses generally came in at or below budget,” and added “sometimes the rounding also will get you.”

  • Wesley Golladay, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: asked about cap rate mix given “mid- to high 7s” commentary; President, CEO & Director Constant said equity raised broadened deal availability “in and around that 7.5%,” enabling blending with “high 7s approaching 8,” while Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said “that’s the range we’ve been operating in and around for quite some time.”

  • Wesley Golladay, Robert W. Baird & Co. Incorporated: asked full-year G&A; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said it should be “right around $20 million.”

  • Jana Galan, BofA Securities: asked about the $125 million pipeline split and development timelines; Chief Investment Officer & Senior VP Ryan said it is “tilted towards the development funding,” generally “that 3- to 12-month time horizon.”

  • Michael Gorman, BTIG, LLC: asked about rent coverage bucket movements; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman said “The short answer is no,” citing a three-month lag (data through 12/31) and that results were “very similar, very consistent, very stable quarter-over-quarter.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, with risk-focused questions on cost of capital, credit loss, 7-Eleven closures, gas-price volatility, cap rate direction, and rent coverage category shifts.

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, with confident liquidity framing and pipeline language, including “we are in a very strong capital position” (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman) and “we are confident that we can deploy this capital accretively” (President, CEO & Director Constant).

  • Versus last quarter: the prior call emphasized the CIO transition and a $100 million under-contract pipeline; the current call emphasized guidance being raised and expanded under-contract pipeline, while Q&A focus shifted more toward macro (gas prices) and tenant-specific headlines (7-Eleven) alongside capital strategy.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance moved to “$2.50 to $2.52” from the prior “$2.48 to $2.50,” while management reiterated the same framework that guidance excludes prospective investments and capital markets activity. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Investment posture broadened from “approximately $100 million of investments under contract” (prior quarter) to “approximately $125 million of investments under contract” and additional liquidity detail including “more than $170 million of unsettled forward equity” and an undrawn revolver. (President, CEO & Director Constant)

  • Leadership change referenced last quarter (RJ Ryan succeeding Mark Olear as CIO) became the new steady-state in the current quarter, with Ryan delivering portfolio and pipeline detail as Chief Investment Officer. (President, CEO & Director Constant)

Risks and concerns

  • Management highlighted “volatility driven by current geopolitical events” and “geopolitical and macro noise,” while maintaining that rent collections were 100% and tenant performance remained resilient. (President, CEO & Director Constant; Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Credit loss risk was addressed via a “25 basis points assumption for credit loss,” with management stating no Q1 credit loss and no watch-list issues. (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Dickman)

  • Sector-specific pressure points raised in Q&A included fuel price spikes and tenant rationalization (7-Eleven closures); management’s mitigation framing centered on fuel margin pass-through and portfolio evolution toward “the modern C-store.” (President, CEO & Director Constant)

Final takeaway

Getty’s management described Q1 2026 as a strong start, pointing to rent and per-share earnings growth alongside a higher full-year AFFO per share range of $2.50 to $2.52. Executives emphasized liquidity (including forward equity and an undrawn revolver) to support a growing investment pipeline, while characterizing tenant credit as healthy and maintaining a conservative credit loss assumption despite macro and geopolitical volatility, fuel-price concerns, and convenience-store format shifts such as 7-Eleven’s store closures.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Esquire Financial outlines ~5.90% net interest margin outlook through year-end as Signature deal progresses
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Esquire Financial Holdings (ESQ) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “For the current quarter, we printed GAAP net income of $12.2 million, or $1.40 per diluted share,” and “excluding these two items, our adjusted net income was $13.8 million, or $1.58 per diluted share.” (Senior VP & CFO Michael Lacapria)

  • “Our net interest margin remained resilient at 604 basis points,” and “loan growth on a linked quarter basis was $56.7 million, or 13% annualized, reaching $1.82 billion.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • “As it relates to our litigation loan portfolio, we saw $44 million, or 15% annualized net growth, bringing our litigation book to $1.22 billion at a yield of approximately 9% for the quarter,” while “deposit growth on a linked-quarter basis was $39.6 million or 8% annualized, where our total deposits reached $2.1 billion at a cost of funds inclusive of demand remaining flat at 1%.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • “We have zero exposure to commercial office space or construction and vacant land loans,” and “we foreclosed on the properties securing our $7.8 million non-accrual multifamily loan… recognizing a $3.2 million net charge-off.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • “We’ve made strong progress on the Signature merger to date, including filing all regulatory applications, filing our Form S-4 with the SEC,” and “we believe… that the Signature merger is transformational for us.” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Andrew Sagliocca)

Outlook

  • The company did not provide EPS or revenue guidance in the prepared remarks; management focused on margin expectations, liquidity posture, and pro forma capacity following the Signature deal.

  • “We see the NIM on average being around 5.90-ish low, call it 5.90%, through the end of the year,” and “then we see another 10 basis points in ’27.” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca)

  • On pro forma lending capacity tied to the Signature acquisition, management said, “our legal lending limit significantly increasing from right around $40 million to as much as $70 million or $80 million on a pro forma basis.” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca)

Financial Results

  • “Non-interest income was stable at $6.5 million, or 16% of total revenue,” and payment processing “services 93,000 small business clients and processed $9.7 billion across 137 million transactions this quarter.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • “Adjusted operational expenses of $19 million were in line with the trailing quarter, driving an industry-leading adjusted efficiency ratio of 46.9%,” and “our capital foundation is strong and well-capitalized, with equity to assets of 12.44% and bank-level regulatory leverage and CET1 ratios at 11.85% and 14.25% respectively.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • “From a corporate perspective, we increased our regular quarterly cash dividend by 14% to $0.20 per share paid this past March.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

Q&A

  • Thomas Reid, Raymond James: asked about Fortress JV scalability post-Signature; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca said, “the relationship with Fortress is going well,” adding that with Signature “the need for them would be less logically,” while emphasizing Fortress as “a good business partner… on longer duration-type inventories… mass tort and class actions.”

  • Thomas Reid, Raymond James: questioned whether the payment processing business is still core and asked about divestiture; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca responded, “No, it is absolutely core to the overall strategy,” and “we have no plans on divesting of it,” while adding, “we will continue to look towards doing direct business with merchants, especially with the pending Signature merger.”

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about Signature integration and litigation-lending ramp and training; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca said, “the integration is going really well. The reception has been outstanding,” and added, “we plan on discussing planning towards and the like prior to closing,” with training focused on joint law-firm visits: “that’s the best on-the-job training you could ask for.”

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about net interest margin trajectory assuming no rate cuts; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca said, “we see the NIM on average being around 5.90-ish low… through the end of the year,” with “another 10 basis points in ’27.”

  • Timothy Switzer, KBW: asked about deploying excess deposits and timing; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca said, “any excess liquidity can be deployed fairly quickly,” and added, “I’d rather have the NIM compress a little bit and have a lot of dry powder on the balance sheet.”

  • Bader Hijleh, Piper Sandler: asked about litigation pipeline and whether demand is accelerating or decelerating; Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca said, “I don’t see it decelerating,” and described the pipeline as “very robust,” adding, “it’s closer to a high water mark.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral and detail-oriented, centering on concentration and scalability (Fortress JV; litigation growth lumpiness), strategic optionality (payments divestiture), and balance-sheet trajectory (NIM, liquidity), including “How should we think about the NIM trajectory going forward?” (Timothy Switzer, KBW).

  • Management’s tone was confident and expansion-focused, using categorical language on strategic assets and growth runway, including “No, it is absolutely core to the overall strategy” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca) and “I don’t see it decelerating” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca).

  • A quarter-over-quarter tone comparison was not possible because the prior-quarter transcript was not provided beyond “not available.”

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The previous-quarter transcript was not available in the provided materials, so changes in guidance language, strategy emphasis, and Q&A focus versus last quarter could not be corroborated from transcripts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged merger-related costs and governance changes, citing “$1.3 million… merger costs associated with our acquisition of Signature Bancorporation, Inc.” and “$398,000 in accelerated stock compensation expense related to the previously announced departure of two board members.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • Credit risk and resolution activity included “non-performing loans totaling $736,000,” and a resolved problem credit where the company “recogniz[ed] a $3.2 million net charge-off.” (Senior VP & CFO Lacapria)

  • Liquidity and margin trade-offs were explicitly acknowledged: “I’d rather have the NIM compress a little bit and have a lot of dry powder on the balance sheet.” (Vice Chairman, CEO & President Sagliocca)

Final Takeaway

Esquire’s leadership highlighted Q1 profitability and a still-high net interest margin while emphasizing litigation lending momentum, strong asset quality positioning, and a liquidity-first posture. Management framed the pending Signature transaction as “transformational,” with integration work underway and pro forma capacity expanding the bank’s legal lending limit to “as much as $70 million or $80 million,” while also outlining an internal NIM expectation of about “5.90%… through the end of the year.”

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. soldier charged after allegedly earning $400,000 betting on Maduro’s removal
data.description:
U.S. Army soldier Gannon Ken Van Dyke allegedly earned over $400,000 on Polymarket by trading with classified information about the timing of a U.S. military operation to capture Nicolás Maduro in Venezuela.

The Justice Department announced Thursday the unsealing of an indictment charging Van Dyke with "unlawful use of confidential government information for personal gain, theft of nonpublic government information, commodities fraud, wire fraud, and making an unlawful monetary transaction."

The charges arise from an alleged scheme in which Van Dyke used sensitive classified information to make wagers on Polymarket (POLYMARKET), a prediction marketplace.

As alleged in the indictment, Van Dyke participated in the planning and execution of the U.S. military operation to capture Maduro, and used his access to classified information about that operation to personally profit. 

“Today’s announcement makes clear no one is above the law, and this FBI will do whatever it takes to defend the homeland and safeguard our nation’s secrets,” said FBI Director Kash Patel. “Any clearance holders thinking of cashing in their access and knowledge for personal gain will be held accountable.”

“Van Dyke profited more than $400,000 by trading various outcomes related to Venezuela after learning of the operation because of his role as a U.S. Army soldier,"  said FBI Assistant Director in Charge James C. Barnacle Jr. 

As alleged, on or about Dec. 26, 2025, Van Dyke created a Polymarket account, funded it, and began trading on Maduro- and Venezuela-related markets. In total, Van Dyke made approximately 13 bets from Dec. 27, 2025, through the evening of Jan. 26. Those bets all took the “YES” position on “U.S. Forces in Venezuela . . . by January 31, 2026”; “Maduro out by . . . January 31, 2026”; “Will the U.S. invade Venezuela by . . . January 31,”; or “Trump invokes War Powers against Venezuela by . . . January 31,” the DOJ statement said.

Van Dyke bet a total of about $33,034 on those outcomes while in possession of classified nonpublic information about the operation.

Prediction market platform Kalshi (KALSHI) meanwhile said on Wednesday that it has suspended and  penalized three congressional candidates - from Minnesota, Texas, and Virginia - over alleged “political insider trading” tied to their campaigns.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: MaxLinear outlines $160M-$170M Q2 revenue outlook as it lifts 2026 optical data center target to $150M-$170M
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: MaxLinear (MXL) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Q1 was a strong and important start to the year, and we believe it marks the beginning of a multiyear growth phase for MaxLinear, led by our optical data center business." (Co-Founder, Chairman, CEO & President Kishore Seendripu)

  • "Based on customer orders and rising visibility of the program ramps, we are increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "Keystone is now ramping at multiple major hyperscale customers across both the U.S. and Asia, supporting 400G and 800G -- 800G PAM4 deployments for scale-up and scale-out applications." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "We anticipate production ramps beginning in late 2026" for Rushmore/Annapurna, with "revenue growth expected to continue strong growth through 2027." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "We have secured our first XGS-PON design win at a U.S. hyperscale data center through a Tier 1 OEM partner" and also "won USB bridge controller designs with two major hyperscalers." (CEO Seendripu)

  • "Total revenue for the first quarter was $137.2 million" and the company saw infrastructure at "approximately $63 million" alongside broadband "approximately $44 million." (CFO & Chief Corporate Strategy Officer Steven Litchfield)

Outlook

  • "We currently expect revenue in the second quarter of 2026 to be between $160 million and $170 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "We expect second quarter GAAP gross margin to be approximately 56% to 59% and non-GAAP gross margin to be in the range of 58% and 61% of revenue." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "We expect Q2 2026 GAAP operating expenses to be in the range of $91 million to $97 million" and "non-GAAP operating expenses to be in the range of $61 million to $66 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • Versus the prior quarter’s positioning for Keystone, management moved from "about $100 million to $130 million in revenue in '26" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call) to "increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range" (CEO Seendripu, Q1 call).

Financial Results

  • "Total revenue for the first quarter was $137.2 million, up from $136.4 million in the previous quarter" and "up 43% from the $95.5 million in the first quarter of 2025." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins for the first quarter was 57.5% and 59.5% of revenue" and "the delta ... was primarily driven by $2.6 million of acquisition-related intangible asset amortization." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "First quarter GAAP operating expenses were $96.1 million and non-GAAP operating expenses were $59.9 million" with the gap "primarily due to stock-based compensation and performance-based equity accruals of $28.5 million combined" and "acquisition-related costs and other costs of $6.5 million." (CFO Litchfield)

  • "In Q1, net cash flow used in operating activities was approximately $8.9 million" and "we exited Q1 of 2026 with approximately $89.9 million in cash, cash equivalents and restricted cash" as the "primary use of cash" was "substantial prepayment for wafers supporting rising demand" tied to "increasing order backlog in the second half of the year." (CFO Litchfield)

Q&A

  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: asked what drove the optical DSP outlook step-up; CEO Seendripu: "we were being conservative" and now "we have very good visibility and the ramps are setting in very nicely" across "400-gig and 800-gig solutions."

  • Tore Svanberg, Stifel: asked if wafer prepayments are done and about the revolver; CFO Litchfield: "we knew we would have some working capital needs" and "it depends on how much demand continues to improve"; on the facility, "we renewed the revolver" as it "was expiring in June" and "took it up slightly."

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: pressed on supply/capacity; CFO Litchfield: "there's supply constraints out there" but "we planned well for this" and "worked really closely with the partners."

  • Joseph Quatrochi, Wells Fargo: questioned margin leverage vs. the revenue step-up; CFO Litchfield: "it's a little bit of the input cost" with "wafer costs, packaging" moving up, adding "given the uncertainty out there, I think we just want to remain cautious."

  • Christopher Rolland, Susquehanna: asked if hyperscaler language implies concentration; CEO Seendripu: "It is pretty broad-based" with designs "across all the module vendors" and "we are only halfway there" on hyperscaler diversification.

  • Richard Shannon, Craig-Hallum: asked broadband trajectory and DOCSIS 4.0 timing; CEO Seendripu: "we expect our broadband business to continue to start growing from Q2" and "a big growth is coming with Ultra DOCSIS 3.1 and 4.0 into 2027" while "operators are still delayed on their network readiness."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly positive, with repeated emphasis on durability and composition of the ramp, customer concentration, supply readiness, and why margins are not stepping up faster (e.g., "what seems like is a pretty meaningful change" from Rolland; "Why wouldn't we see maybe a little bit more leverage" from Quatrochi).

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and generally confident in Q&A, while using caution language on costs and visibility (e.g., "we are increasing our expectations" (CEO Seendripu) alongside "we just want to remain cautious" (CFO Litchfield)).

  • Versus the previous quarter, management’s stance on optical ramp visibility strengthened (Q4: "potential upside" and emphasis on lead times; Q1: "we have very good visibility" and raised 2026 optical data center revenue expectations), while analyst probing shifted toward concentration and margin leverage as revenue inflects.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • The core narrative moved from "we expect Keystone to generate about $100 million to $130 million in revenue in '26" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call) to "increasing our expectations for 2026 optical data center revenue to $150 million to $170 million range" (CEO Seendripu, Q1 call).

  • Q4 framed capital returns and balance sheet confidence ("we repurchased $20 million worth of our common stock" (CEO Seendripu, Q4 call)); Q1 discussion pivoted to funding growth with working capital actions, including wafer prepayments as demand rises ("substantial prepayment for wafers" (CFO Litchfield)).

  • Analysts in Q1 spent more time challenging gross margin sensitivity and mix-through timing, while Q4 questions were more centered on optical market dynamics (LPO/CPO) and general growth direction.

Risks and Concerns

  • "There's supply constraints out there" and costs are moving up across "wafer costs, packaging" with caution embedded in gross margin guidance. (CFO Litchfield)

  • Cash usage was tied to scaling supply for demand: "substantial prepayment for wafers" amid "increasing order backlog in the second half of the year." (CFO Litchfield)

  • On broadband timing, management cited operator delays: "some of the operators are still delayed on their network readiness" for DOCSIS 4.0-related upgrades. (CEO Seendripu)

Final Takeaway

MaxLinear’s management described Q1 as the start of a "multiyear growth phase" led by optical data center, raised its 2026 optical data center revenue expectations to $150 million-$170 million, and guided Q2 revenue to $160 million-$170 million. In Q&A, analysts focused on the drivers behind the optical step-up, supply readiness and working-capital impacts, and why gross margin guidance remains cautious despite the sequential revenue increase, while management reiterated improved program visibility, ongoing input-cost uncertainty, and a continued push to broaden hyperscaler diversification as ramps expand into 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: DeepSeek unveils new AI model, targets OpenAI and Anthropic
data.description:
• DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) has released preview versions of a new flagship AI model, a year after shaking up Silicon Valley, describing it as the most powerful open-source platform in a bid to challenge rivals like OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic (ANTHRO).
  • The Chinese startup unveiled the V4 Flash and V4 Pro preview series, touting top-tier performance in coding benchmarks and big advancements in reasoning and agentic tasks.
  • They come with several architecture upgrades and optimization improvements and can operate with a million-token context length, the startup said on Hugging Face.
  • The Hangzhou-based AI startup ignited a trillion-dollar stock market selloff in 2025 with the release of the R1, an open-source model that mimics the process of human reasoning.

data.title: Matson declares $0.36 dividend and 3M share additional buyback
data.description:
• Matson (MATX) declares $0.36/share quarterly dividend, in line with previous.
  • Forward yield 0.85%
  • Payable June 4; for shareholders of record May 7; ex-div May 7.
  • "We are pleased to announce an additional three million shares to our existing share repurchase program," said Matt Cox, Matson's Chairman and Chief Executive Officer.
  • "Since we commenced our share repurchase program in August 2021, we have repurchased approximately 14.3 million shares, or approximately 33% of the then outstanding shares, for a total cost of $1.3 billion." he added.
  • See MATX Dividend Scorecard, Yield Chart, & Dividend Growth.
